walkthrough.title: Unfold the Factory Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Kanban EV set up. First, unfold the large factory board and place it in the center of the table. This board depicts five departments arranged vertically: Design at the top, then Logistics, Assembly, Research and Development, and Testing and Innovation at the bottom. Every action in the game happens on this board, so make sure everyone can reach it.

walkthrough.title: Place the Meeting Board
walkthrough.text: Now find the meeting board and set it next to the factory board. This smaller board tracks Sandra's schedule and the upcoming meetings. It shows which department Sandra currently occupies and when the next meeting will be triggered. Meetings are a huge part of scoring, so keep this board visible to all players.

walkthrough.title: Choose Sandra's Difficulty
walkthrough.text: Before going any further, your group needs to agree on Sandra's difficulty level. Friendly Sandra is more forgiving during meetings and rewards accomplishments without harsh penalties. Tough Sandra punishes players who fall behind and creates much more competitive pressure. For your first game, we strongly recommend Friendly Sandra.

walkthrough.title: Place Sandra on the Board
walkthrough.text: Find the Sandra meeple. She is the distinctive factory manager figure. Place her in the starting department indicated by the scenario you are playing. Sandra will roam through departments on a predictable schedule, observing workers and triggering meetings. Her starting position matters because she will evaluate anyone working in her department.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Shift Clock
walkthrough.text: Locate the shift clock and place it on the factory board in its designated area. Set the marker to the starting position. The shift clock advances each time a player takes a turn. When the clock completes a full cycle, Sandra moves and eventually triggers a meeting. This clock is the heartbeat of the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Stock the Design Department
walkthrough.text: Let's fill the factory with starting materials. In the Design department at the top of the board, place the starting car design cards face up in the available slots. These blueprints show different electric vehicle models and specify which parts each car requires. Players will visit this department to acquire blueprints for the cars they want to build.

walkthrough.title: Stock the Logistics Department
walkthrough.text: Now move to the Logistics department. Place the starting part tiles in their designated spaces. Parts include batteries, motors, seats, and chassis components. Each part tile has a specific type, and you will need the right combination of parts to match a car design. Arrange them so every player can see what is currently available.

walkthrough.title: Stock Assembly and Remaining Departments
walkthrough.text: Set up the Assembly department with any starting car tiles in progress. Then stock the Research and Development department with innovation upgrade tokens. Finally, prepare the Testing and Innovation department with its starting certification tokens. Each department should have its initial supply as shown in the rulebook setup diagram.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Week Track
walkthrough.text: Place the week marker on space one of the week track. The number of weeks in the game depends on your player count. With fewer players, you play more weeks so everyone gets enough turns. The week track tells you exactly how many meetings will happen before the final scoring round.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Supply
walkthrough.text: Sort the victory point tokens, extra part tiles, and car tiles into organized piles near the factory board. Place the training cubes and certification tokens in a general supply area. These components will flow onto the board and into player hands throughout the game, so keep them accessible but tidy.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Boards
walkthrough.text: Give each player a personal garage board. This board tracks the cars you have completed and the certifications you have earned. You will also track your training level in each of the five departments here. Place your garage board in front of you where everyone can see your progress.

walkthrough.title: Take Your Worker and Training Cubes
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a worker meeple in their chosen color. You also take training cubes and place them at the bottom level of each department track on your player board. Everyone starts untrained in all five departments. You will advance these cubes during the game to unlock more powerful actions.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Depending on turn order, each player receives a small starting allocation of parts or designs. The first player gets fewer starting resources because they have the advantage of acting first. Later players receive slightly more to compensate. Check the rulebook chart for exact distributions based on your player count.

walkthrough.title: Place Workers in Starting Departments
walkthrough.text: Each player places their worker meeple in one of the five factory departments. You may choose any department as your starting location. Think about what you want to accomplish on your very first turn because you must move to a different department when your turn comes. Choose wisely.

walkthrough.title: Determine Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Randomly determine who goes first. Turn order in Kanban EV can shift during the game based on where players position themselves in the factory. For now, assign a starting order and make sure everyone knows who goes when. You are ready to start producing electric vehicles.

walkthrough.title: The Core Concept
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how Kanban EV works. You are a worker in an electric vehicle factory, competing to impress Sandra, the demanding factory manager. Over several work weeks, you will move between five departments to design cars, acquire parts, assemble vehicles, research upgrades, and test finished products. Sandra roams the factory and periodically holds meetings where she evaluates everyone's performance.

walkthrough.title: The Game Arc
walkthrough.text: The game spans multiple work weeks, with the exact number determined by your player count. Each week consists of several shifts tracked by the shift clock. When the clock cycles, Sandra moves and eventually calls a meeting. Meetings are the primary scoring events in the game. The final meeting triggers the endgame and last scoring round.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Move Your Worker
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you must move your worker meeple to a different department. You cannot stay in the same department where you currently are. Pick any one of the other four departments and place your worker there. Where you move determines what action you can take this turn.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Perform the Department Action
walkthrough.text: After moving, you perform the action of the department you moved to. Each department offers a unique action tied to the car production pipeline. We will cover all five department actions in detail over the next few steps. Your training level in that department affects how powerful your action is.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Sandra Check
walkthrough.text: If Sandra happens to be in the same department as you when you work there, she observes your performance. With Friendly Sandra, this is mostly a bonus opportunity. With Tough Sandra, being caught underperforming in her presence can result in penalties. Always check where Sandra is before you move.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Advance the Shift Clock
walkthrough.text: After your action, advance the shift clock by one tick. When the clock completes a full cycle, it triggers the end of a work day. After enough days pass, Sandra calls a meeting. This clock drives the tempo of the entire game, so keep an eye on how many ticks remain before the next meeting.

walkthrough.title: Department Action: Design
walkthrough.text: When you visit the Design department, you acquire car blueprints. These design cards specify exactly which parts a car needs to be assembled. Higher training in Design lets you access better or more designs per visit. Collecting the right designs early gives you a clear plan for which parts to gather next.

walkthrough.title: Department Action: Logistics
walkthrough.text: The Logistics department is where you pick up parts. You can acquire batteries, motors, seats, and chassis components. Your training level determines how many parts you can take and which quality of parts you can access. Match the parts you collect to the designs you already hold so nothing goes to waste.

walkthrough.title: Department Action: Assembly
walkthrough.text: Visit Assembly when you have a design card and all the required parts. Combine them to build a completed car, which moves to your personal garage on your player board. Higher training in Assembly can reduce the number of parts needed. Assembled cars are critical for scoring at meetings.

walkthrough.title: Department Action: Research and Development
walkthrough.text: The Research and Development department lets you apply innovation upgrades to car designs. Upgraded cars score more victory points during meetings and at game end. Training in this department gives you access to better upgrades. This department is optional but can significantly boost the value of each car you produce.

walkthrough.title: Department Action: Testing and Innovation
walkthrough.text: Testing and Innovation is where you test completed cars for victory points and earn certifications. Certifications prove your expertise in specific departments and are evaluated during meetings. This department is essential because certifications are a major scoring category that many beginners overlook.

walkthrough.title: Training and Its Benefits
walkthrough.text: Each time you work in a department, you may have the opportunity to advance your training there. Training cubes move up a track on your player board. Higher training unlocks more powerful versions of department actions. For example, higher Logistics training lets you take more parts per visit, and higher Assembly training reduces part requirements.

walkthrough.title: The Car Production Pipeline
walkthrough.text: Let's connect it all together. The core loop is a four-step pipeline. First, visit Design to get a blueprint. Second, visit Logistics to collect the required parts. Third, visit Assembly to build the car. Fourth, optionally visit Testing to score the car and earn certifications. You can also slip in an Research and Development visit to upgrade a design before assembling it.

walkthrough.title: Sandra's Movement Pattern
walkthrough.text: Sandra moves through departments on a predictable schedule shown on the meeting board. She follows a set path, so you can always see where she will be in one or two turns. Smart players plan their department visits to either align with Sandra when they are productive or avoid her when they are not ready to impress.

walkthrough.title: Meetings: How They Work
walkthrough.text: When the shift clock triggers a meeting, everyone stops and Sandra evaluates all players. During meetings, Sandra checks your completed cars, certifications, training levels, and innovation contributions. Based on these factors, you earn or lose victory points. Meetings are the biggest scoring opportunities in the game.

walkthrough.title: Meetings: Friendly vs. Tough Sandra
walkthrough.text: With Friendly Sandra, meetings focus on rewarding what you have accomplished. She gives partial credit and nobody gets punished too harshly. With Tough Sandra, players who fall behind face real penalties. She compares players against each other and the lowest performers can lose points. Choose the version that matches your group's competitiveness.

walkthrough.title: Garage Management
walkthrough.text: Completed cars go to your personal garage on your player board. The cars in your garage are evaluated at every meeting and at game end. Having a diverse collection of different car models is usually worth more than building duplicates. Upgraded cars with Research and Development improvements score even higher.

walkthrough.title: Overtime Shifts
walkthrough.text: Some actions allow you to take overtime, which gives you a bonus action at a cost. Overtime can be powerful for squeezing in an extra move before a meeting. However, overusing overtime leads to diminishing returns. Use it strategically when the timing is critical, not as a crutch every turn.

walkthrough.title: Lean Manufacturing Principles
walkthrough.text: Kanban EV rewards just-in-time production. Taking only the parts you need for your current design is more efficient than stockpiling resources. The factory tracks car production across departments, and bottlenecks in the system affect everyone. Think lean and avoid waste whenever possible.

walkthrough.title: End-of-Week Effects
walkthrough.text: At the end of each work week, certain factory conditions reset. Fresh designs and new parts become available in the departments. The week marker advances, bringing you closer to the next meeting and ultimately the end of the game. Use week boundaries to plan your long-term strategy.

walkthrough.title: Certifications Explained
walkthrough.text: Certifications are earned primarily through the Testing department. They represent your proven expertise in specific areas. During meetings, Sandra checks how many certifications each player has. Certifications also provide end-game scoring bonuses. Building a diverse set of certifications across multiple departments is highly valuable.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Final Meeting
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the last scheduled meeting, which is determined by the player count and week track. This final meeting works the same as every other meeting but is your last chance to score. After final scoring, add up all your victory points from meetings, completed cars, certifications, training levels, and set bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Categories
walkthrough.text: Your final score comes from five main sources. Meeting scores accumulated throughout the game. Victory points for each completed car in your garage, with upgraded cars scoring more. Certifications earned. Bonus points for high training levels. And set bonuses for having diverse car collections or certifications across multiple departments.

walkthrough.title: Determining the Winner
walkthrough.text: The player with the most total victory points wins. In the event of a tie, the player with more completed cars in their garage takes the victory. Now that you know the rules, let's walk through a practice round to see everything in action.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Factory
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first shift at the Kanban EV factory. In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted sequence of turns with three example players. Player one is focused on fast car production. Player two is chasing upgrades and innovation. Player three is spreading across departments for certifications. Follow along to see how the game flows.

walkthrough.title: Starting Positions
walkthrough.text: Everyone has placed their worker in a starting department. Player one starts in Design, planning to grab a blueprint right away. Player two starts in Research and Development, eyeing early upgrades. Player three starts in Logistics, ready to stockpile some parts. Sandra is in the Assembly department, watching and waiting.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Visit Design
walkthrough.text: Player one moves to Design and picks up a car blueprint. This is a simple sedan design requiring two parts: one battery and one motor. It is a low-complexity car, perfect for an early build. Player one now has a clear plan: head to Logistics next for the parts, then straight to Assembly.

walkthrough.title: Why Start with Design
walkthrough.text: Grabbing a design first is smart because it gives you a specific shopping list. Without a blueprint, you are guessing at which parts to collect. Player one knows exactly what they need: one battery and one motor. No wasted trips, no wasted parts. This is lean manufacturing in action.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Visit Design
walkthrough.text: Player two also moves to Design this turn and picks up a different blueprint. This one is a luxury SUV requiring three parts: one battery, one motor, and one seat. It is a more complex build but will score higher. Player two plans to upgrade this design in Research and Development before assembling it.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Visit Logistics
walkthrough.text: Player three moves from Logistics to Design to grab a blueprint. Wait, actually, Player three realizes they should have grabbed a design first before collecting parts. They already picked up a battery on their starting turn, but without a blueprint, they are not sure what else they need. This is our first beginner mistake.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Collecting Parts Without a Plan
walkthrough.text: Here is the lesson. Never collect parts without a design in hand. If you grab random parts from Logistics, you might end up with components that do not match any available blueprint. You have wasted a turn collecting something you cannot use. Always visit Design first, then Logistics.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Visit Logistics
walkthrough.text: Player one moves to Logistics and collects the battery and motor needed for their sedan design. Because Player one has low training in Logistics, they can only take two parts this visit, which is exactly what they need. Perfect efficiency. Player one is now ready to assemble on their next turn.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Visit Research and Development
walkthrough.text: Player two moves to Research and Development and applies an innovation upgrade to their SUV design. This upgrade will add bonus victory points when the car is completed and scored at meetings. The upgrade costs a turn but pays dividends later. Player two is playing the long game.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Visit Design (Recovery)
walkthrough.text: Player three recovers from their mistake by visiting Design to grab a blueprint. They find a compact hatchback that conveniently needs a battery, which they already have, plus one seat. Player three only needs one more part to complete this car. The earlier misplay cost a turn of efficiency but is not a disaster.

walkthrough.title: Sandra Moves
walkthrough.text: The shift clock has advanced enough that Sandra moves from Assembly to the Design department. Any player who visits Design next turn will be observed by Sandra. Player one is relieved because they were heading to Assembly anyway. Player three needs to decide whether to risk working under Sandra's gaze.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring Sandra's Position
walkthrough.text: Let's say Player three ignores Sandra's position and visits a department without considering the consequences. With Tough Sandra, this can mean penalties if you show up without accomplishments to display. Always track Sandra on the meeting board. Plan your moves so you encounter her when you have something impressive to show, like a freshly assembled car or a new certification.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Assemble a Car
walkthrough.text: Player one moves to Assembly with their sedan design and both required parts. They combine the design card, the battery, and the motor to build the car. The completed sedan moves to Player one's personal garage. That is one car ready for the next meeting. Player one is in great shape.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Hoarding Parts Before Meetings
walkthrough.text: Here is another common beginner mistake. Some new players stockpile three or four sets of parts, thinking they will assemble everything at once. But you can only visit one department per turn. If a meeting triggers while your parts are sitting unassembled, they score you nothing. Sandra evaluates completed cars in your garage, not parts in your hand. Assemble cars as soon as you can.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Visit Logistics
walkthrough.text: Player two heads to Logistics to collect the three parts needed for their upgraded SUV. With basic training, they can take two parts this visit. They grab the battery and the motor but still need the seat. Player two will need another Logistics trip or will need to level up their training to grab all three at once.

walkthrough.title: The Value of Training
walkthrough.text: Notice how Player two's low training in Logistics limited them to two parts. If they had higher training, they could have grabbed all three in one visit and saved a turn. Training is an investment. Spending early turns advancing your training in key departments pays off hugely in the mid and late game.

walkthrough.title: Meeting Triggers
walkthrough.text: The shift clock is about to complete its cycle. A meeting is coming. Let's check everyone's status. Player one has one completed car. Player two has an upgraded design but no completed car yet. Player three has parts and a design but nothing assembled. Only Player one is truly ready for this meeting.

walkthrough.title: The First Meeting
walkthrough.text: Sandra calls the meeting. She evaluates everyone's accomplishments. Player one scores well for having a completed car in their garage. Player two earns partial credit for the innovation upgrade but loses potential points for having no finished car. Player three scores poorly with nothing assembled and no certifications. Meetings punish unfinished work.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Neglecting Certifications
walkthrough.text: None of the three players visited Testing before this meeting. That means nobody earned any certifications, and Sandra checks for those during evaluations. Certifications are easy to overlook because they feel less urgent than building cars. But they add up to significant points across multiple meetings and at game end. Make Testing part of your regular rotation.

walkthrough.title: Post-Meeting Strategy
walkthrough.text: After the meeting, everyone regroups. Player one plans to build a second car before the next meeting. Player two rushes to finish their upgraded SUV. Player three finally commits to assembling their hatchback and visiting Testing for certifications. Everyone adjusts their strategy based on meeting results.

walkthrough.title: Building Momentum
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, your training levels increase and department actions become more powerful. Player one can now grab three parts from Logistics in a single visit. Player two's Research and Development training unlocks premium upgrades. Player three starts earning certifications that score at every future meeting. The mid-game is where engines start humming.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Continue taking turns, moving to departments, performing actions, and watching the shift clock. Keep an eye on Sandra's position and the meeting schedule. Plan two or three turns ahead so you have completed cars and certifications ready when meetings hit. The rhythm of build, score, build, score will become natural.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations on completing your first practice round of Kanban EV. You have seen the car production pipeline in action, learned the importance of meeting preparation, and witnessed three common beginner mistakes. Remember: get a design before parts, assemble cars before meetings, and do not neglect certifications.

walkthrough.title: Before You Clock In
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on department focus and Sandra management.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to department actions and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun building electric vehicles!

walkthrough.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, let's cover the mistakes that trip up new players the most. First, hoarding parts instead of assembling cars before meetings. Parts in your hand score nothing. Second, ignoring Sandra's position and getting caught unprepared in Tough mode. Third, neglecting the Testing department and missing out on certifications. Fourth, spreading training too thin across all five departments instead of focusing on two or three.

walkthrough.title: Focus Your Training
walkthrough.text: You cannot master all five departments in one game. Pick two or three departments that form a production pipeline and invest your training there. A common beginner path is Design, Logistics, and Assembly. This gives you strong blueprints, efficient part collection, and reduced assembly costs. Add Testing once you have cars rolling off the line. Trying to level up all five tracks equally will leave you mediocre everywhere and competitive nowhere.

walkthrough.title: Plan Around Meetings
walkthrough.text: Meetings are the heartbeat of scoring in Kanban EV. Always know how many shift clock ticks remain until the next meeting. Work backwards from that deadline. If a meeting is three turns away, make sure you can complete and garage at least one car in that time. Walking into a meeting with an empty garage and no certifications is the fastest way to fall behind.

walkthrough.title: Manage Sandra
walkthrough.text: Sandra's movement is predictable, and you should use that to your advantage. With Friendly Sandra, try to be in her department when you have something to show off, like a freshly assembled car or a new certification. She will reward you with bonus points. With Tough Sandra, avoid her department unless you are confident in your performance. Getting caught idle or unproductive in her presence costs you dearly.

walkthrough.title: Build Cars Before Stockpiling
walkthrough.text: It is tempting to collect multiple designs and hoard parts for a big assembly spree. Resist that urge. Complete one car at a time, get it into your garage, and then start the next one. A finished car scores at every remaining meeting, so the earlier you build it, the more times it earns you points. An unassembled pile of parts earns exactly zero, no matter how impressive it looks.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Skip Certifications
walkthrough.text: New players often skip Testing because building cars feels more productive. But certifications score at every meeting and at game end. They are one of the highest-value per-turn investments in the game. Plan to visit Testing at least two or three times during a full game. Aim for certifications across different departments for the diversity bonus.

walkthrough.title: Adapt to Other Players
walkthrough.text: Watch what departments other players are visiting. If everyone is crowding Logistics, the parts will be picked over by the time your turn comes. If nobody is visiting Research and Development, you can snag the best upgrades unopposed. Flexibility is key. Have a plan but be willing to adjust your route through the factory based on the competition.

walkthrough.title: Use Overtime Wisely
walkthrough.text: Overtime lets you squeeze in an extra action, but it comes at a cost. The best time to use overtime is right before a meeting when you need one more turn to finish a car or earn a certification. The worst time is early in the game when you have no pressing deadline. Think of overtime as an emergency tool, not a routine strategy. Save it for the moments that matter most.

summary.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
bullets: Don't hoard parts — unassembled parts score 0 at meetings
Don't ignore Sandra's position — penalties in Tough mode
Don't skip Testing — certifications are high-value scoring
Don't spread training across all 5 depts — focus on 2-3

bullets: Pick 2-3 departments forming a production pipeline
Common beginner path: Design → Logistics → Assembly
Add Testing once cars are rolling
Equal training in all 5 = mediocre everywhere

summary.title: Plan Around Meetings
bullets: Always know how many clock ticks until next meeting
Work backwards from deadline — can you finish 1 car in time?
Empty garage + no certs at meeting = fastest way to fall behind

bullets: Sandra's movement is predictable — use it
Friendly: be in her dept with accomplishments → bonus points
Tough: avoid her unless you're confident in your output

bullets: Complete 1 car at a time, get it garaged, start the next
Earlier cars score at more meetings → compound value
Unassembled parts = 0 points no matter how many

bullets: Certs score at every meeting AND at game end
Visit Testing 2-3 times per game minimum
Diverse certs across departments → diversity bonus

summary.title: Adapt to Other Players
bullets: Watch which departments opponents are visiting
Crowded dept = picked-over resources by your turn
Uncontested dept = best picks for you

summary.title: Use Overtime Wisely
bullets: Best use: right before a meeting for a critical last action
Worst use: early game with no pressing deadline
Treat as emergency tool, not routine strategy

walkthrough.title: Speed Builder Strategy
walkthrough.text: The speed builder approach focuses on maximizing the number of completed cars. Invest heavily in Design, Logistics, and Assembly training to streamline the production pipeline. With high training, you can grab better designs, collect more parts per visit, and reduce assembly costs. The goal is to have the most cars in your garage at every meeting. Quantity beats quality when Sandra checks your output.

walkthrough.title: Executing the Speed Build
walkthrough.text: Start by grabbing a simple design on turn one. Visit Logistics on turn two for parts, and assemble on turn three. Repeat this three-turn cycle as quickly as possible. Invest training in Logistics first because collecting more parts per visit eliminates the bottleneck. Once you can grab all required parts in a single trip, your cycle drops to just three departments per car. This strategy dominates early meetings when other players are still setting up.

walkthrough.title: Innovation Specialist Strategy
walkthrough.text: The innovation specialist builds fewer cars but makes each one significantly more valuable through Research and Development upgrades. Invest training in Research and Development and Assembly to access premium upgrades and reduce costs. Each upgraded car scores substantially more at meetings and endgame. This strategy trades speed for per-car efficiency and works best in longer games with more meetings.

walkthrough.title: Certification Hunter Strategy
walkthrough.text: The certification hunter prioritizes Testing and cross-department training to earn maximum certifications. Certifications score at every meeting and at game end, creating compounding value. Build just enough cars to stay competitive at meetings, then pour remaining turns into Testing. A player with five or six diverse certifications can outscore a speed builder over the course of a full game.

walkthrough.title: Sandra Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Advanced players treat Sandra's schedule as a tactical resource rather than an obstacle. Study her movement pattern at the start of the game and plan your entire route around it. With Friendly Sandra, deliberately position yourself in her department when you assemble a car or earn a certification for maximum bonus points. With Tough Sandra, use her as a weapon by avoiding her while opponents stumble into her path unprepared.

walkthrough.title: Training Investment Timing
walkthrough.text: Training early is an investment with compounding returns. Every subsequent visit to a trained department is more powerful. The optimal timing is to invest in training during the first two weeks when meetings are still far off and the pressure is low. By mid-game, your trained departments should be running at full efficiency. Avoid training in the final week because there are not enough remaining turns to recoup the investment.

walkthrough.title: Meeting Tempo Control
walkthrough.text: The shift clock advances every turn, and some advanced plays involve controlling the tempo leading into meetings. If you are ready for a meeting but opponents are not, try to accelerate the clock by taking efficient single-department actions. If you need more time, look for opportunities that slow your pace without wasting turns. Reading the table and knowing when each opponent is prepared gives you a significant edge.

walkthrough.title: Hybrid Approach and Adaptability
walkthrough.text: The strongest Kanban EV players do not lock into one pure strategy. They start with a plan but adapt based on what designs are available, where opponents are going, and where Sandra is headed. A typical winning game might start with speed building for the first meeting, then pivot to upgrades and certifications for later meetings. Read the board state every turn and ask yourself where the highest-value action is right now, not three turns from now.

summary.title: Speed Builder Strategy
bullets: Maximize completed car count via Design → Logistics → Assembly pipeline
Invest training in Logistics + Assembly for efficiency
Quantity of cars → strong meeting performance
Dominates early meetings while others are setting up

Strongest when meetings come early and often

5-6 diverse certs can outscore a speed builder over full game

entries.definition: The first department in the car production pipeline. Visit here to acquire car blueprint/design cards that specify which parts a car requires. Higher training grants access to better or more designs per visit.

The department where you collect parts needed to build cars. Available parts include batteries, motors, seats, and chassis components. Training level determines how many and what quality of parts you can take per visit.

The department where you combine a car design with all required parts to build a completed car. The car moves to your personal garage. Higher training can reduce the number of parts required for assembly.
The department where you apply innovation upgrades to car designs before assembly. Upgraded cars score more victory points at meetings and at game end. Training unlocks access to better upgrades.
The department where you test completed cars for victory points and earn certifications. Certifications prove expertise and are evaluated during meetings. A major but often overlooked scoring source.

The factory manager who roams through departments on a predictable schedule. She observes workers in her current department and periodically calls meetings to evaluate everyone's performance. Her difficulty level (Friendly or Tough) changes how meetings play out.

The easier difficulty mode. Sandra rewards accomplishments during meetings without harsh penalties. She gives partial credit and focuses on what you have done rather than punishing what you have not. Recommended for first games.

The harder difficulty mode. Sandra penalizes players who fall behind during meetings. She compares players against each other and the lowest performers can lose victory points. Creates more competitive pressure.

A periodic evaluation event triggered by the shift clock. Sandra checks all players' completed cars, certifications, training levels, and innovation contributions. Meetings are the primary scoring events in the game.

The last meeting of the game, determined by player count and the week track. It works like a normal meeting but is the last chance to score. After the final meeting, all remaining end-game bonuses are tallied.

A timer on the factory board that advances one tick each time a player takes a turn. When it completes a full cycle, it triggers Sandra's movement and eventually a meeting. The shift clock drives the tempo of the entire game.
Tracks how many work weeks have passed. The total number of weeks in a game depends on player count. The week track determines how many meetings will occur before the final scoring round.

Each player's piece that moves between departments on the factory board. You must move your worker to a different department each turn. Your worker's location determines which department action you perform.

The distinctive figure representing Sandra, the factory manager. Placed on the factory board in a department. Moves on a predictable schedule shown on the meeting board. Her position affects player interactions and scoring.

The central game board depicting five departments arranged vertically: Design, Logistics, Assembly, R&D, and Testing & Innovation. All department actions and most game interactions take place on this board.
A secondary board placed next to the factory board. Tracks Sandra's current position, her movement schedule, and upcoming meeting timing. Essential for planning your turn sequence.
Each player's personal board tracking completed cars, certifications earned, and training levels in all five departments. Cars in your garage are evaluated at meetings and at game end.

A card acquired from the Design department showing a specific car model and the parts required to build it. You need one design card plus all listed parts to assemble a car. Designs can be upgraded via R&D.

Tiles representing components needed to assemble cars, including batteries, motors, seats, and chassis. Collected from the Logistics department. Parts must match the requirements on your car design card.

Represents a completed electric vehicle in your garage. Created by combining a design card with all required parts in the Assembly department. Different car models come in different colors and have different scoring values.

Earned through the Testing and Innovation department. Certifications represent proven expertise in specific areas. Evaluated by Sandra during meetings and worth victory points at game end. Diverse certifications earn bonus points.
Small cubes placed on department tracks on your player board. Start at the bottom (untrained) and advance as you work in departments. Higher training levels unlock more powerful department actions.
Innovation upgrade tokens acquired from the Research and Development department. Applied to car designs before assembly to increase the car's scoring value at meetings and end of game.

Tokens used to track victory points earned during meetings and from other scoring events. The player with the most VP at game end wins. VP sources include meetings, cars, certifications, training, and set bonuses.

Your expertise in a specific department, tracked by training cubes on your player board. Higher levels grant more powerful actions when visiting that department. Ranges from untrained (bottom) to fully trained (top of track).

The specific action you perform when you move your worker to a department. Each of the five departments has a unique action. The strength of the action depends on your training level in that department.
An optional extra action available on some turns at a cost. Useful for squeezing in a critical action before a meeting. Overuse leads to diminishing returns. Best used sparingly as a strategic tool.

The four-step process of building a car: Design (get blueprint) → Logistics (collect parts) → Assembly (build car) → Testing (score and certify). An optional R&D step can be added before Assembly to upgrade the design.

Sandra's predictable movement pattern through departments, displayed on the meeting board. She moves when the shift clock cycles. Players should plan department visits around her schedule for bonuses or to avoid penalties.

When Sandra is in the same department as a player, she observes their work. With Friendly Sandra, this can trigger approval bonuses. With Tough Sandra, being observed while unproductive can result in penalties.

A game design principle in Kanban EV that rewards just-in-time production. Taking only the parts you need for your current design is more efficient than hoarding. Waste is penalized and efficiency is rewarded.
Having different car models in your garage rather than duplicates. Diverse car collections typically score set bonuses at meetings and game end, making variety more valuable than repetition.
Victory points earned during each meeting based on Sandra's evaluation of your cars, certifications, training, and innovations. Meeting scores are the largest source of VP in most games.
Victory points scored for each car in your garage at game end. Upgraded cars with R&D improvements score higher than base models. Car VP is scored both during meetings and as an end-game category.
Victory points earned from certifications at meetings and game end. Having certifications across multiple different departments earns diversity bonuses worth additional VP.
Bonus victory points awarded at game end for high training levels in departments. Players with advanced training in multiple departments earn more end-game VP from this category.

Additional victory points earned for having diverse collections — either different car models in your garage or certifications across multiple departments. Set bonuses reward breadth rather than depth.
If two or more players finish with the same total VP, the player with the most completed cars in their garage wins. Used only when final scores are exactly equal.
One of the part types collected from the Logistics department. Required by most car designs as a core power component. A battery tile is one of the most commonly needed parts in the game.
One of the part types collected from the Logistics department. Required by most car designs as the drive component. Along with batteries, motors are among the most frequently needed parts.

One of the part types collected from the Logistics department. Required by many car designs, especially higher-complexity models. Seats are often the third part added to upgrade from a basic to a mid-range vehicle.
One of the part types collected from the Logistics department. Required by certain car designs, typically the more complex or luxury models. Chassis components are often the hardest parts to obtain.
